title: Project 2
text:
This project involved the analysis and visualisation of a dataset using Power BI. The dataset was loaded, cleaned, and explored to develop an interactive dashboard. A range of visualisations were created to examine key variables, relationships, and distributions.
The analysis identified meaningful patterns and trends, which were used to develop evidence-based recommendations. Results were compiled into a written report including an executive summary and supporting visuals, alongside a presentation incorporating the Power BI dashboard.




Power BI
Microsoft Word
Microsoft PowerPoint




An interactive dashboard was developed that clearly communicated key insights, trends, and patterns across the dataset variables.



This project developed my ability to create professional data visualisations and communicate analytical findings clearly to a business audience.
Back to Projects

title: Welcome to My E-Portfolio
text:
Welcome to my e-portfolio! I’m Cáit, a final year Business Analytics student at DCU.
Use the navigation bar above to explore my work.

title: About Me
text:
Student • Analytical • Creative
I first developed an interest in Business Analytics through my long-standing passion for problem solving, mathematics, and working with computers. These interests naturally aligned when I began studying Business at Dublin City University (DCU), where I am specialising in Data Analytics. From early in my degree, I was drawn to the analytical side of business, enjoying the challenge of using data to understand patterns, improve decision-making, and solve real-world problems.
During first year, my exposure to Business Analytics tools such as Excel, Jamovi, and Tableau further strengthened my interest in the field. The hands-on and practical nature of analytics modules stood out to me, allowing me to move beyond theory and actively work with data. These experiences confirmed my desire to pursue analytics as a career path and motivated me to continue developing my technical and analytical skill set.
In my third year, I completed an 11-month placement at PwC as a Risk Assurance Internal Auditor. This role provided valuable exposure to a professional services environment and allowed me to apply analytical thinking in a real-world business context. Throughout my placement, I worked with data to assess risk, evaluate controls, and support internal audit engagements, gaining a strong understanding of governance, risk, and assurance processes. The experience strengthened my attention to detail, problem-solving abilities, and professional communication skills.
Now in my final year at DCU, I continue to build on my analytical foundation through my specialisation in Data Analytics, working with SQL, Python, CSS and HTML. I am particularly motivated by the opportunity to combine business knowledge with data-driven insights and technology. With a strong academic background, practical industry experience, and a genuine passion for analytics, I am eager to pursue a career where I can continue learning and delivering value through data.
GitHub LinkedIn

title: Project 1
text:
This assignment involved the statistical analysis of a House Price dataset using Jamovi. The analysis was structured into three main sections: descriptive statistics and correlations, statistical hypothesis testing, and linear regression modelling.
Descriptive statistics were used to summarise the House Price variable. Correlation analysis examined relationships between house price and other quantitative variables. Statistical tests investigated differences in house prices across cities and house types. Linear regression modelled house prices using size, bedrooms, bathrooms, and land area as predictors.




Jamovi
Microsoft Word




Two regression models were developed. The first used house size, number of bedrooms, bathrooms, and land area. The second used statistically significant predictors from the first model alongside additional relevant variables.



This project strengthened my understanding of statistical testing, correlation analysis, and regression modelling in a real-world dataset context.
Back to Projects

Jamovi

title: Project 3
text:
This analysis was divided into two parts. Part A focused on confidence interval construction using the House Price dataset. Part B examined sampling distributions using an external dataset on accident costs.
In Part A, three confidence intervals (90%, 95%, 99%) were calculated for average house price. A 95% confidence interval was calculated for average house size in Cork, and another for the proportion of detached houses sold in Ireland. The required sample size to reduce sampling error to 0.02 was also calculated.
In Part B, an Excel dataset on accident costs was analysed to compare individual accident cost distributions across 1,000 companies with average costs from 200 samples of 100 companies, with reference to the Central Limit Theorem.




Jamovi
Microsoft Excel
Microsoft Word




The analysis demonstrated how confidence level affects interval width, and how averaging across samples reduces variability in line with the Central Limit Theorem.



This project deepened my understanding of inferential statistics, confidence intervals, and sampling theory.
Back to Projects
